title: Thiruvananthapuram

infobox.value: [Adv V.V. Rajesh](/wiki/V._V._Rajesh "V. V. Rajesh") ([BJP](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party"))

* KL-01 Thiruvananthapuram * KL-22 [Kazhakootam](/wiki/Kazhakootam "Kazhakootam") * KL-15 [KSRTC](/wiki/Kerala_State_Road_Transport_Corporation "Kerala State Road Transport Corporation") * KL-16 [Attingal](/wiki/Attingal "Attingal") * KL-19 [Parassala](/wiki/Parassala "Parassala") * KL-20 [Neyyattinkara](/wiki/Neyyattinkara "Neyyattinkara") * KL-21 [Nedumangad](/wiki/Nedumangad "Nedumangad") * KL-74 [Kattakkada](/wiki/Kattakkada "Kattakkada") * KL-81 [Varkala](/wiki/Varkala "Varkala")
[Thumba Equatorial Rocket Launching Station](/wiki/Thumba_Equatorial_Rocket_Launching_Station "Thumba Equatorial Rocket Launching Station")

(Malayalam pronunciation: [[t̪iɾuʋənən̪d̪ɐˈbuɾəm]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Malayalam "Help:IPA/Malayalam") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Trivandrum.ogg "File:Trivandrum.ogg") [*tirr-ROO-və-nun-Tə-poor-əm*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), also known as **Trivandrum**,[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-districtprofile-10) is the [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") city of the Indian state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"). As of 2011, the [Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Municipal_Corporation "Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation") had a population of 957,730 over an area of 214.86 km2,[[11]](#cite_note-citypop-11) making it the largest and most populous city in Kerala.[[10]](#cite_note-districtprofile-10) The larger [Thiruvananthapuram metropolitan area](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_metropolitan_area "Thiruvananthapuram metropolitan area") has over 1.7 million inhabitants within an area of 543 km2.[[12]](#cite_note-uapop-12) Thiruvananthapuram is one of the few cities in India that functions as a capital city,[[13]](#cite_note-13) a heritage city,[[14]](#cite_note-14) a maritime city,[[15]](#cite_note-15) an information technology city,[[16]](#cite_note-16) a space research city,[[17]](#cite_note-17) a defence city, an automotive tech city,[[18]](#cite_note-18) a bioscience city,[[19]](#cite_note-19) a [tourism city](/wiki/Tourist_city "Tourist city"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) and a city known for its research and development institutions. It is also among the few cities in the world where both an [international airport](/wiki/Trivandrum_International_Airport "Trivandrum International Airport") and an [international seaport](/wiki/Vizhinjam_International_Seaport_Thiruvananthapuram "Vizhinjam International Seaport Thiruvananthapuram") are located within the city in close proximity to the city center.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Metropolis and capital of Kerala, India

This article is about the city. For the district, see [Thiruvananthapuram district](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_district "Thiruvananthapuram district"). For the urban agglomeration area of Thiruvananthapuram, see [Thiruvananthapuram metropolitan area](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_metropolitan_area "Thiruvananthapuram metropolitan area").

**Thiruvananthapuram** (Malayalam pronunciation: [[t̪iɾuʋənən̪d̪ɐˈbuɾəm]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Malayalam "Help:IPA/Malayalam") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Trivandrum.ogg "File:Trivandrum.ogg") [*tirr-ROO-və-nun-Tə-poor-əm*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), also known as **Trivandrum**,[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-districtprofile-10) is the [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") city of the Indian state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"). As of 2011, the [Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Municipal_Corporation "Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation") had a population of 957,730 over an area of 214.86 km2,[[11]](#cite_note-citypop-11) making it the largest and most populous city in Kerala.[[10]](#cite_note-districtprofile-10) The larger [Thiruvananthapuram metropolitan area](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_metropolitan_area "Thiruvananthapuram metropolitan area") has over 1.7 million inhabitants within an area of 543 km2.[[12]](#cite_note-uapop-12) Thiruvananthapuram is one of the few cities in India that functions as a capital city,[[13]](#cite_note-13) a heritage city,[[14]](#cite_note-14) a maritime city,[[15]](#cite_note-15) an information technology city,[[16]](#cite_note-16) a space research city,[[17]](#cite_note-17) a defence city, an automotive tech city,[[18]](#cite_note-18) a bioscience city,[[19]](#cite_note-19) a [tourism city](/wiki/Tourist_city "Tourist city"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) and a city known for its research and development institutions. It is also among the few cities in the world where both an [international airport](/wiki/Trivandrum_International_Airport "Trivandrum International Airport") and an [international seaport](/wiki/Vizhinjam_International_Seaport_Thiruvananthapuram "Vizhinjam International Seaport Thiruvananthapuram") are located within the city in close proximity to the city center.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Located on the west coast of India near the extreme south of the mainland, Thiruvananthapuram is a port city located 10 [nautical miles](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile") (19 km; 12 mi)[[22]](#cite_note-22) from a heavily trafficked east–west shipping channel. The city is home to India's first deep-water trans-shipment port, the [Vizhinjam International Seaport Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Vizhinjam_International_Seaport_Thiruvananthapuram "Vizhinjam International Seaport Thiruvananthapuram").[[23]](#cite_note-23) The city is characterised by its undulating terrain of low coastal hills.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Thiruvananthapuram is also known for its cultural heritage, being associated with the musical contributions of [Swathi Thirunal Rama Varma](/wiki/Swathi_Thirunal "Swathi Thirunal") and the artistic legacy of painter [Raja Ravi Varma](/wiki/Raja_Ravi_Varma "Raja Ravi Varma"). Thiruvananthapuram has contributed to the development of Malayalam literature through individuals like [Ulloor S. Parameswara Iyer](/wiki/Ulloor_S._Parameswara_Iyer "Ulloor S. Parameswara Iyer"), [Kumaran Asan](/wiki/Kumaran_Asan "Kumaran Asan"), [C. V. Raman Pillai](/wiki/C._V._Raman_Pillai "C. V. Raman Pillai") and [Narayana Guru](/wiki/Narayana_Guru "Narayana Guru"). The city is also known for [Sree Padmanabhaswamy Temple](/wiki/Sree_Padmanabhaswamy_Temple "Sree Padmanabhaswamy Temple"), known as the richest temple in the world.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The present regions that constitute Thiruvananthapuram were ruled by the [Ays](/wiki/Ay_kingdom "Ay kingdom") who were related to feudatories of the [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty").[[26]](#cite_note-vizhis3-26) In the 12th century, it was conquered by the [Kingdom of Venad](/wiki/Venad "Venad").[[26]](#cite_note-vizhis3-26) In the 18th century, the king [Marthanda Varma](/wiki/Marthanda_varma "Marthanda varma") expanded the territory, founded the princely state of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") and made Thiruvananthapuram its capital.[[27]](#cite_note-history1-27) [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") became the most dominant state in Kerala by defeating the powerful [Zamorin](/wiki/Zamorin "Zamorin") of [Calicut](/wiki/History_of_Calicut "History of Calicut") in the battle of [Purakkad](/wiki/Purakkad "Purakkad") in 1755.[[28]](#cite_note-AHoT_162-28) Following [India's independence](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") in 1947, Thiruvananthapuram became the capital of [Travancore–Cochin](/wiki/Travancore%E2%80%93Cochin "Travancore–Cochin") state and remained so until the new Indian state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") was formed in 1956.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Thiruvananthapuram is a notable academic and research hub and home to the [University of Kerala](/wiki/University_of_Kerala "University of Kerala"), [APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University](/wiki/APJ_Abdul_Kalam_Technological_University "APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University"), the regional headquarters of [Indira Gandhi National Open University](/wiki/Indira_Gandhi_National_Open_University "Indira Gandhi National Open University"), and many other schools and colleges. Thiruvananthapuram is also home to research centres such as the [National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Interdisciplinary_Science_and_Technology "National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology"), [Indian Space Research Organisation](/wiki/Indian_Space_Research_Organisation "Indian Space Research Organisation")'s [Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre](/wiki/Vikram_Sarabhai_Space_Centre "Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre"), the [Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology](/wiki/Indian_Institute_of_Space_Science_and_Technology "Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology"), [National Centre for Earth Science Studies](/wiki/National_Centre_for_Earth_Science_Studies "National Centre for Earth Science Studies") and a campus of the [Indian Institutes of Science Education and Research](/wiki/Indian_Institutes_of_Science_Education_and_Research "Indian Institutes of Science Education and Research").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Thiruvananthapuram is where India's space program began, with the headquarters of Liquid Propulsion Systems Centre located there. The city is home to media institutions like Toonz Animation India and [Tata Elxsi Ltd](/wiki/Tata_Elxsi "Tata Elxsi"), and also to [Chitranjali Film Studio](/wiki/Chitranjali_Studio "Chitranjali Studio"), one of the first film studios in [Malayalam Cinema](/wiki/Malayalam_Cinema "Malayalam Cinema"), and [Kinfra Film and Video Park](/wiki/Kinfra_Film_and_Video_Park "Kinfra Film and Video Park") at [Kazhakoottam](/wiki/Kazhakoottam "Kazhakoottam"), which is India's first infotainment industrial park.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In 2012, Thiruvananthapuram was named the best Kerala city to live in, by a field survey conducted by *[The Times of India](/wiki/The_Times_of_India "The Times of India")*.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In 2013, the city was ranked the fifteenth best city to live in India, in a survey conducted by *[India Today](/wiki/India_Today "India Today")*.[[33]](#cite_note-India_Today_survey-33) Thiruvananthapuram was ranked the best Indian city for two consecutive years, 2015 and 2016, according to the Annual Survey of India's City-Systems (ASICS) conducted by the [Janaagraha](/wiki/Janaagraha "Janaagraha") Centre for Citizenship and Democracy.[[34]](#cite_note-Best_Indian_City-34) The city was also selected as the best governed city in India in a survey conducted by Janaagraha Centre for citizenship and democracy in 2017.[[35]](#cite_note-Best_Governed_City-35)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The city takes its name from the [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") word *thiru-anantha-puram* (തിരുവനന്തപുരം [[t̪iɾuʋɐnɐn̪d̪ɐpuɾɐm]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Malayalam "Help:IPA/Malayalam") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-Thiruvananthapuram.oga "File:En-Thiruvananthapuram.oga")), meaning "The City of Lord Ananda",[[36]](#cite_note-Meaning_of_name-36) referring to the [deity](/wiki/Deity "Deity") of the [Sri Padmanabhaswamy Temple](/wiki/Sri_Padmanabhaswamy_Temple "Sri Padmanabhaswamy Temple") located in the city. Thiruvananthapuram is also known in literature, and popular reference as *Ananthapuri*, derived from the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") word *Syanandurapuram*, meaning "The City of Bliss" in Carnatic kirtanas composed by [Swathi Thirunal](/wiki/Swathi_Thirunal "Swathi Thirunal"), erstwhile Maharaja of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore").[[37]](#cite_note-37) The city was officially referred to as *Trivandrum* until 1991 (Trivandrum being the anglicised name of the town), when the government decided to reinstate the city's original name Thiruvananthapuram.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The city was also called as *Anandapatnam* or *Anandapatam* in [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") and [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") languages meaning "The City of Ananda" which is the same to meaning for the original name.

### Pre Historic Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pre Historic Period")]

Thiruvananthapuram is a relatively modern region with trading traditions dating back to 1000 BCE.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) It is believed that the ships of [King Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon") landed in a port called [Ophir](/wiki/Ophir "Ophir") (now [Poovar](/wiki/Poovar "Poovar")) in Thiruvananthapuram in 1036 BCE.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) The city was the trading post of [spices](/wiki/Spices "Spices"), [sandalwood](/wiki/Sandalwood "Sandalwood") and [ivory](/wiki/Ivory "Ivory").[[43]](#cite_note-Ancient_Trade_in_Thiruvananthapuram-43) However, the ancient political and cultural history of the city was almost entirely independent from that of the rest of Kerala.

### Ancient Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ancient Period")]

The southern region of present-day Kerala state (The coastal belt between Thiruvananthapuram and [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha "Alappuzha")) was under [Ay dynasty](/wiki/Ay_dynasty "Ay dynasty"), who was more related to the [Pandya dynasty](/wiki/Pandya_dynasty "Pandya dynasty") of [Madurai](/wiki/Madurai "Madurai").[[44]](#cite_note-44) The early rulers of the city were the [Ays](/wiki/Ay_kingdom "Ay kingdom"). [Vizhinjam](/wiki/Vizhinjam "Vizhinjam"), which is now a region in the present-day Thiruvananthapuram, was the capital of the Ay dynasty.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Vizhinjam was an important port city from as early as the second century BC.[[46]](#cite_note-vizhis-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) During the Ay dynasty's rule, Thiruvananthapuram witnessed many battles in which the [Chola](/wiki/Chola_dynasty "Chola dynasty") and [Pandyan](/wiki/Pandyan_dynasty "Pandyan dynasty") dynasties attempted to capture the port town.[[48]](#cite_note-vizhis1-48)[[49]](#cite_note-vizhis2-49)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

After the death of king [Vikramaditya Varaguna](/wiki/Vikramaditya_Varaguna "Vikramaditya Varaguna") in 925 AD, the glory of the Ays departed and almost all their territories became part of the [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty").[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[26]](#cite_note-vizhis3-26) During the tenth century, the Cholas attacked and sacked Vizhinjam and surrounding regions.[[26]](#cite_note-vizhis3-26) The port in Vizhinjam and the historic education center of Kanthalloor Sala were also destroyed by Cholas during this period.[[51]](#cite_note-kanthalloor-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) A branch of the Ay family, which had controlled the [Padmanabhaswamy Temple](/wiki/Padmanabhaswamy_Temple "Padmanabhaswamy Temple"), merged with the [Kingdom of Venad](/wiki/Venad "Venad") in the 12th century.[[26]](#cite_note-vizhis3-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/1733_Homann_Heirs_Map_of_India_-_Geographicus_-_India-homannheirs-1733.jpg/250px-1733_Homann_Heirs_Map_of_India_-_Geographicus_-_India-homannheirs-1733.jpg)](/wiki/File:1733_Homann_Heirs_Map_of_India_-_Geographicus_-_India-homannheirs-1733.jpg)

A map of [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast") drawn by Homann Heirs in 1733. At that time, Travancore was only a small territory wedged between [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam") and [Kanyakumari](/wiki/Kanyakumari "Kanyakumari"), as shown in the map (Present-day districts of [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_district "Thiruvananthapuram district") and Kanyakumari only).

### Early Modern Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early Modern Period")]

It was in the later half of the 18th century, that Travancore inherited the kingdoms up to [Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") and became a powerful kingdom, and Thiruvananthapuram became a major city of Kerala.

Present-day Thiruvananthapuram city, district, and [Kanyakumari district](/wiki/Kanyakumari_district "Kanyakumari district"), were parts of the [Ay dynasty](/wiki/Ay_dynasty "Ay dynasty") during ancient and [medieval ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), in the southernmost part of the Indian subcontinent.[[53]](#cite_note-askh-53) Ay kingdom had experienced attacks and conquests by [Cholas](/wiki/Chola_dynasty "Chola dynasty") and [Pandyas](/wiki/Pandya_dynasty "Pandya dynasty") in various periods.[[53]](#cite_note-askh-53) Later it became a part of [Venad](/wiki/Venad "Venad") in late Middle Ages, which was eventually expanded as the powerful kingdom of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") in 18th century CE.[[53]](#cite_note-askh-53) The [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_people "Tamil people")-Dravidian kind of architecture is also found in Padmanabhaswamy temple, which makes it distinct and unique from the architectural style of temples in northern and central parts of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala").[[53]](#cite_note-askh-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Thambiran_Vanakkam_1578.JPG/250px-Thambiran_Vanakkam_1578.JPG)](/wiki/File:Thambiran_Vanakkam_1578.JPG)

*[Thambiran Vanakkam](/wiki/Thambiran_Vanakkam "Thambiran Vanakkam")* was printed at Kollam, the capital of [Venad](/wiki/Venad "Venad") in 1578, during the Portuguese Era. It holds the record of the first book printed in any Indian language. It was written in the language *[Lingua Malabar Tamul](/wiki/Lingua_Malabar_Tamul "Lingua Malabar Tamul")* which was spoken in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") during the medieval period.

### Late Modern Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Late Modern Period")]

In the early 18th century CE, the [Travancore royal family](/wiki/Travancore_royal_family "Travancore royal family") adopted some members from the royal family of [Kolathunadu](/wiki/Kolathunadu "Kolathunadu") based at [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur").[[54]](#cite_note-54) Then, [Marthanda Varma](/wiki/Marthanda_Varma "Marthanda Varma") who inherited the Kingdom of Venad expanded the kingdom by conquering the kingdoms of Kayamkulam, Kottarakara, Kottayam, Changanassery, Meenachil, Poonjar and Ambalapuzha.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In 1729, [Marthanda Varma](/wiki/Marthanda_Varma "Marthanda Varma") founded the princely state of [Thiruvithamkoor](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") and Thiruvananthapuram was made the capital in 1795 after shifting the capital from [Padmanabhapuram](/wiki/Padmanabhapuram "Padmanabhapuram") in [Kanyakumari](/wiki/Kanyakumari "Kanyakumari") district.[[56]](#cite_note-district_history-56) Thiruvananthapuram became a prominent city in Kerala under [Marthanda Varma](/wiki/Marthanda_Varma "Marthanda Varma").[[57]](#cite_note-Online._Encyclopædia_Britannica_Inc_2013-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/A_language_map_of_India_prepared_for_the_missionary_projects_at_Serampore%2C_1822.jpg/250px-A_language_map_of_India_prepared_for_the_missionary_projects_at_Serampore%2C_1822.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_language_map_of_India_prepared_for_the_missionary_projects_at_Serampore,_1822.jpg)

A language map of India prepared in 1822.

As a result of the annexation of neighbouring [chiefdoms](/wiki/Chiefdom "Chiefdom"), the artists and scholars from these places migrated to Thiruvananthapuram, turning it into a cultural center. Marthanda Varma gave patronage to different temple art forms including *Koothu*, *Padhakam*, *Kathakali*, *Thullal*, and *Koodiyattam*. Noted artists such as [Ramapurathu Warrier](/wiki/Ramapurathu_Warrier "Ramapurathu Warrier") and [Kunchan Nambiar](/wiki/Kunchan_Nambiar "Kunchan Nambiar") amongst others served as his court poets.[[58]](#cite_note-Gauri_Lakshmi_Bayi_1998_152–168-58) Travancore became the most dominant state in Kerala by defeating the powerful [Zamorin](/wiki/Zamorin "Zamorin") of [Kozhikode](/wiki/History_of_Kozhikode "History of Kozhikode") in the battle of [Purakkad](/wiki/Purakkad "Purakkad") in 1755.[[28]](#cite_note-AHoT_162-28)

The city developed into a significant intellectual and artistic centre during this period. The city's golden age was during the mid-19th century under the reign of [Maharaja Swathi Thirunal](/wiki/Swathi_Thirunal_Rama_Varma "Swathi Thirunal Rama Varma") and [Maharaja Ayilyam Thirunal](/wiki/Ayilyam_Thirunal "Ayilyam Thirunal"). This era saw the establishment of the first English school (1834), the [Observatory](/wiki/Trivandrum_Observatory "Trivandrum Observatory") (1837), the General Hospital (1839), [the Oriental Research Institute & Manuscripts Library](/wiki/The_Oriental_Research_Institute_%26_Manuscripts_Library "The Oriental Research Institute & Manuscripts Library") and the University College (1873). The first mental hospital in the state was started during the same period. [Sanskrit College](/wiki/Sanskrit_College "Sanskrit College"), Ayurveda College, [Law College](/wiki/Law_College "Law College") and a second-grade college for women were started by [Moolam Thirunal](/wiki/Moolam_Thirunal "Moolam Thirunal") (1885–1924).[[59]](#cite_note-History_of_Thiruvananthapuram-59)

### Contemporary Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Contemporary Period")]

The early 20th century was an age of tremendous political and social changes in the city. The [Sree Moolam Popular Assembly](/wiki/Sree_Moolam_Popular_Assembly "Sree Moolam Popular Assembly"), established in 1904, was the first democratically elected legislative council in any Indian state.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Despite not being under the direct control of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") at any time, the city featured prominently in [India's freedom struggle](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement"). The [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") had a very active presence in Thiruvananthapuram. A meeting of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") presided by Dr [Pattabhi Sitaramaiah](/wiki/Pattabhi_Sitaramaiah "Pattabhi Sitaramaiah") was held here in 1938.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Palace_of_Trivandrum.jpg/250px-Palace_of_Trivandrum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palace_of_Trivandrum.jpg)

[Kowdiar Palace](/wiki/Kowdiar_Palace "Kowdiar Palace") built-in 1915 was the official residence of the [Travancore Royal Family](/wiki/Travancore_Royal_Family "Travancore Royal Family").[[61]](#cite_note-Kowdiar-61)

The Thiruvananthapuram Municipality came into existence in 1920 as the first municipality in the [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") region. The municipality was converted into a corporation on 30 October 1940, during the period of [Chitra Thirunal Bala Rama Varma](/wiki/Chitra_Thirunal_Bala_Rama_Varma "Chitra Thirunal Bala Rama Varma"), who took over in 1931.[[62]](#cite_note-ThiruvananthapuramHistoryIntro-62) The city witnessed multi-faceted progress during his period. The promulgation of the "[Temple Entry Proclamation](/wiki/Temple_Entry_Proclamation "Temple Entry Proclamation")" (1936) was an act that underlined social emancipation. This era also saw the establishment of the [University of Travancore](/wiki/Kerala_University "Kerala University") in 1937, which later became [Kerala University](/wiki/Kerala_University "Kerala University").[[63]](#cite_note-University_of_Kerala_History-63)

### Modern Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Modern Period")]

With the end of British rule in 1947, Travancore chose to join the Indian union. The first popularly elected ministry, headed by [Pattom Thanu Pillai](/wiki/Pattom_Thanu_Pillai "Pattom Thanu Pillai"), was installed in office on 24 March 1948. In 1949, Thiruvananthapuram became the capital of [Thiru-Kochi](/wiki/Travancore-Cochin "Travancore-Cochin"), the state formed by the integration of Travancore with its northern neighbour, the [Kochi](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin").[[64]](#cite_note-64) The king of [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore"), [Chitra Thirunal Bala Rama Varma](/wiki/Chitra_Thirunal_Bala_Rama_Varma "Chitra Thirunal Bala Rama Varma"), became the [Rajpramukh](/wiki/Rajpramukh "Rajpramukh") of the [Travancore-Cochin Union](/wiki/Travancore-Cochin "Travancore-Cochin") from 1 July 1949 until 31 October 1956. When the state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") was formed on 1 November 1956, Thiruvananthapuram became its capital.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

With the establishment of [Thumba Equatorial Rocket Launching Station](/wiki/Thumba_Equatorial_Rocket_Launching_Station "Thumba Equatorial Rocket Launching Station") (TERLS) in 1962, Thiruvananthapuram became the cradle of India's ambitious space programme. The first Indian space rocket was developed and launched from the [Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre](/wiki/Vikram_Sarabhai_Space_Centre "Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre") (VSSC) in the outskirts of the city in 1963. Several establishments of the [Indian Space Research Organisation](/wiki/Indian_Space_Research_Organisation "Indian Space Research Organisation") (ISRO) were later established in Thiruvananthapuram.[[66]](#cite_note-VSSC_Thiruvananthapuram-66)

A significant milestone in the city's recent history was the establishment of [Technopark](/wiki/Technopark_Kerala "Technopark Kerala")—India's first IT park—in 1995.[[67]](#cite_note-Technopark_Thiruvananthapuram-67) Technopark has developed into the largest IT park in the geographical area,[[68]](#cite_note-68) employing around 62,000 people in 450 companies.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Geography_of_Thiruvananthapuram "Geography of Thiruvananthapuram")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Pattom_Skyline_panoramo_02.jpg/960px-Pattom_Skyline_panoramo_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pattom_Skyline_panoramo_02.jpg "View of Pattom, the north eastern part of the city. The Western Ghats mountain range is seen in the background")

View of [Pattom](/wiki/Pattom "Pattom"), the north eastern part of the city. The [Western Ghats](/wiki/Western_Ghats "Western Ghats") mountain range is seen in the background

Thiruvananthapuram is built on seven hills[[70]](#cite_note-70) by the seashore and is at [8°30′N 76°54′E﻿ / ﻿8.5°N 76.9°E﻿ / 8.5; 76.9](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Thiruvananthapuram&params=8.5_N_76.9_E_) on the west coast, near the southern tip of mainland India.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The city is on the west coast of India and is bounded by the [Laccadive Sea](/wiki/Laccadive_Sea "Laccadive Sea") to its west and the [Western Ghats](/wiki/Western_Ghats "Western Ghats") to its east. The average elevation of the city is 16 ft (4.9 m) above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level").[[72]](#cite_note-72) The [Geological Survey of India](/wiki/Geological_Survey_of_India "Geological Survey of India") has identified Thiruvananthapuram as a moderately earthquake-prone urban centre and categorised the metropolis in the Seismic III Zone.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Thiruvananthapuram lies on the shores of [Karamana](/wiki/Karamana "Karamana") and [Killi rivers](/wiki/Killi_river "Killi river"). [Vellayani](/wiki/Vellayani_Lake "Vellayani Lake"), [Thiruvallam](/wiki/Thiruvallam "Thiruvallam") and [Aakulam](/wiki/Aakkulam "Aakkulam") backwaters lie in the city.[[74]](#cite_note-74) The soil type in the middle part of the city is a dark brown loamy [laterite soil](/wiki/Laterite "Laterite") high in phosphates. Laterisation is a result of the heavy rainfall and humid conditions. In western coastal regions of the city, [sandy loam](/wiki/Loam "Loam") soil is found, and on hilly eastern parts of the district, rich dark brown loam of granite origin is found.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

The [Thiruvananthapuram Corporation](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Corporation "Thiruvananthapuram Corporation") is spread over 214.86 km2 (82.96 sq mi).[[2]](#cite_note-citygi-2) The wider [Thiruvananthapuram metropolitan area](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_metropolitan_area "Thiruvananthapuram metropolitan area") comprises Thiruvananthapuram corporation, three municipalities and 27 panchayats, as of 2011.[[76]](#cite_note-ListofUA-76) Being the largest city in India's southern tip region, it is essential for both military logistics and civil aviation in the southern part of the country. Thiruvananthapuram is the headquarters of the [Southern Air Command](/wiki/Southern_Air_Command_(India) "Southern Air Command (India)") (SAC) of the [Indian Air Force](/wiki/Indian_Air_Force "Indian Air Force").[[77]](#cite_note-Air_Command-77)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Climate")]

The city has a climate that lies on the border between a [tropical savanna climate](/wiki/Tropical_savanna_climate "Tropical savanna climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Aw*) and a [tropical monsoon climate](/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate") (*Am*). As a result, its only distinct seasons relate to rainfall rather than temperature. Temperatures in Thiruvananthapuram remain remarkably stable throughout the year, with an annual mean maximum of about 31–32 °C (88–90 °F) and a mean minimum around 23–24 °C (73–75 °F). Daytime highs generally stay between 30 and 34 °C (86–93 °F), while nights are warm and humid, averaging approximately 22–25 °C (72–77 °F). The temperature rarely falls below something like both 20 °C (68 °F) and above 35 °C (95 °F). The humidity is high and rises to about 90% during the [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") season.[[78]](#cite_note-Thiruvananthapuram_Weather-78) Thiruvananthapuram is the first city along the path of the [south-west monsoons](/wiki/South-west_monsoon "South-west monsoon") and gets its first showers in early June. The city receives heavy rainfall of around 1,835 mm (72.2 in) per year. The city also gets rain from the receding north-east monsoons which hit the city by October. The dry season sets in by December. The lowest temperature recorded in the city core was 10.8 °C (51.4 °F) on 6 January 1974 and it also recorded 15.6 °C in Jan 18 in 2015. The highest temperature was 38.2 °C (100.8 °F) on 21 February 2019.[[79]](#cite_note-IMDextremes-79) At the airport, the lowest temperature recorded was 12.1 °C (53.8 °F) on 15 January 1975 and the highest temperature was 36.3 °C (97.3 °F) on 5 May 1998.[[79]](#cite_note-IMDextremes-79)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Others include [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") & Other religions (0.06%)

According to provisional results of the 2011 national census, the Corporation of Thiruvananthapuram, which occupies an area of 214 km2 (83 sq mi), had a population of 957,730.[[2]](#cite_note-citygi-2) The city's population density was 4,454/km2 (11,540/sq mi).[[2]](#cite_note-citygi-2) The Urban Agglomeration had a population of 1,687,406 in 2011.[[4]](#cite_note-UApop-4) The sex ratio is 1,040 females for every 1,000 males, which is higher than the national average.[[2]](#cite_note-citygi-2) Thiruvananthapuram's literacy rate of 93.72%[[87]](#cite_note-Citycenpop-87) exceeds the all-India average of 74%.[[88]](#cite_note-censusindliter-88)

It is a historical city where [Malayali](/wiki/Malayalis "Malayalis") form the vast majority of Thiruvananthapuram's population. There are also minorities like the [Tamils](/wiki/Tamil_people "Tamil people") and North Indians residing here. According to the 2011 census, 68.5% of the population is [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), 16.7% [Christians](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") and 13.7% [Muslims](/wiki/Islam "Islam").[[89]](#cite_note-CityCensus-89) The remainder of the community includes [Jains](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh"), [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") and other religions which account for 0.06% of the population; 0.85% did not state a belief in the [census](/wiki/Census "Census").[[89]](#cite_note-CityCensus-89)

[Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), the official state language, is the dominant language in Thiruvananthapuram City:[[90]](#cite_note-90) English is also used, mainly by the white-collar workforce. [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") has the most speakers after [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"). The city also has a few [Tulu](/wiki/Tulu_language "Tulu language"), [Kannada, Konkani](/wiki/Konkani_language "Konkani language"), [Dhivehi](/wiki/Maldivian_language "Maldivian language"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") and Hindi speakers. As per the 2001 census, the population below the [poverty line](/wiki/Below_Poverty_Line_(India) "Below Poverty Line (India)") in the city was 11,667.[[91]](#cite_note-Urban_poor_in_Thiruvananthapuram-91)

Thiruvananthapuram has witnessed massive immigration of workers from northern India, mainly [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"), [Haryana](/wiki/Haryana "Haryana"), and [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh"), and Eastern India, mainly [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") and [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), and from neighbouring countries like [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") and [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh").[[92]](#cite_note-92)

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Administration")]

Main articles: [Administration of Trivandrum district](/wiki/Administration_of_Trivandrum_district "Administration of Trivandrum district") and [Thiruvananthapuram Corporation](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Corporation "Thiruvananthapuram Corporation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly%2C_Thiruvananthapuram.jpg/250px-Kerala_Legislative_Assembly%2C_Thiruvananthapuram.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kerala_Legislative_Assembly,_Thiruvananthapuram.jpg)

[Kerala Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly") Building

The [Corporation of Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Municipal_Corporation "Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation") or TMC oversees and manages the civic infrastructure of the city's 100 wards.[[93]](#cite_note-citad-93) Each ward elects a [councillor](/wiki/Councillor "Councillor") to the Corporation of Thiruvananthapuram. TMC has the power to act as the local government of the city.[[94]](#cite_note-orgst-94) TMC is headed by the Mayor, who is elected from among the [councillors](/wiki/Councillor "Councillor"). The Mayor is responsible for the overall supervision and control of the administrative functions of the TMC. The corporation discharges its services through standing committees.[[94]](#cite_note-orgst-94) The corporation secretary is an officer appointed by the [government](/wiki/Government_of_Kerala "Government of Kerala"), who serves as the administrative head of the TMC and implements the council's decisions based on the resolutions adopted by the council. The functions of the Municipal Corporation are managed by seven departments—engineering, health, general administration, council, accounts and revenue.[[95]](#cite_note-orgstruc-95) For the decentralised role of TMC, eleven Zonal Offices are created. The zonal offices are in Fort, Kadakampally, [Nemom](/wiki/Nemom "Nemom"), [Ulloor](/wiki/Ulloor "Ulloor"), Attipra, Thiruvallom, [Kazhakkuttom](/wiki/Kazhakkuttom "Kazhakkuttom"), [Sreekaryam](/wiki/Sreekaryam "Sreekaryam"), [Kudappanakunnu](/wiki/Kudappanakunnu "Kudappanakunnu"), [Vattiyoorkavu](/wiki/Vattiyoorkavu "Vattiyoorkavu") and [Vizhinjam](/wiki/Vizhinjam "Vizhinjam").[[96]](#cite_note-96) The functions of the TMC include water supply, drainage and sewerage, sanitation, solid-waste management, and building regulation. The [Thiruvananthapuram Development Authority](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Development_Authority "Thiruvananthapuram Development Authority") is responsible for the statutory planning and development of the [greater Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_metropolitan_area "Thiruvananthapuram metropolitan area") region.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

As the seat of the [Government of Kerala](/wiki/Government_of_Kerala "Government of Kerala"), Thiruvananthapuram is home to not only the offices of the local governing agencies but also the [Kerala Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly") and the state secretariat, which is housed in the [Kerala Government Secretariat complex](/wiki/Kerala_Government_Secretariat "Kerala Government Secretariat"). Thiruvananthapuram has two parliamentary constituencies—[Attingal](/wiki/Attingal_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Attingal (Lok Sabha constituency)") and [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_(Lok_Sabha_constituency) "Thiruvananthapuram (Lok Sabha constituency)")—and elects five Members of the Legislative Assembly ([MLAs](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly "Member of the Legislative Assembly")) to the state legislature.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Kerala_Government_Secretariat%2C_Thiruvananthapuram%2C_Kerala%2C_India.jpg/250px-Kerala_Government_Secretariat%2C_Thiruvananthapuram%2C_Kerala%2C_India.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kerala_Government_Secretariat,_Thiruvananthapuram,_Kerala,_India.jpg)

The [Kerala Government Secretariat](/wiki/Kerala_Government_Secretariat "Kerala Government Secretariat") is the seat of administration of the Government of Kerala

### Municipal finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Municipal finance")]

According to financial data published on the CityFinance Portal of the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs,[[99]](#cite_note-99) the Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation reported total revenue receipts of ₹713 crore (US$86 million) and total expenditure of ₹586 crore (US$71 million) in 2022–23. Tax revenue accounted for about 23.6% of the total revenue, while the corporation received ₹476 crore in grants during the financial year.

### Law and order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Law and order")]

The [Thiruvananthapuram City Police](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_City_Police "Thiruvananthapuram City Police") is the main law-enforcement agency in the city. It is headed by a [commissioner of police](/wiki/Police_commissioner "Police commissioner").[[100]](#cite_note-poli-100) The [Thiruvananthapuram city police](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_city_police "Thiruvananthapuram city police") is a division of the [Kerala Police](/wiki/Kerala_Police "Kerala Police"), and the administrative control lies with the [Kerala Home Ministry](/wiki/Department_of_Home_(Kerala) "Department of Home (Kerala)"). Thiruvananthapuram city police is the largest police division in Kerala, and it consists of four subdivisional offices and 24 police stations and a sanctioned strength of 3,500 police personnel.[[101]](#cite_note-kerpol-101) The [Central Prison](/wiki/Central_Prison,_Poojappura "Central Prison, Poojappura") is the oldest prison in Kerala and the headquarters of [Kerala Prisons and Correctional Services](/wiki/Kerala_Prisons_and_Correctional_Services "Kerala Prisons and Correctional Services").[[102]](#cite_note-102)

### Military and diplomatic establishments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Military and diplomatic establishments")]

The [Southern Air Command](/wiki/Southern_Air_Command_(India) "Southern Air Command (India)") of the [Indian Air Force](/wiki/Indian_Air_Force "Indian Air Force") is headquartered in the city.[[103]](#cite_note-SAC-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) There are two state armed police battalions and a unit of the [Central Reserve Police Force](/wiki/Central_Reserve_Police_Force "Central Reserve Police Force") (CRPF) based in Thiruvananthapuram.[[105]](#cite_note-105) The CRPF has a Group Headquarters (GHQ) located at [Pallipuram](/wiki/Pallippuram,_Thiruvananthapuram "Pallippuram, Thiruvananthapuram").[[106]](#cite_note-106) In addition to this, three units of the [Central Industrial Security Force](/wiki/Central_Industrial_Security_Force "Central Industrial Security Force") (CISF) and Sector Headquarters (SHQ) of the [Border Security Force](/wiki/Border_Security_Force "Border Security Force") (BSF) are also present. Thiruvananthapuram also houses a large army cantonment in Pangode which houses some regiments of the [Indian Army](/wiki/Indian_Army "Indian Army").[[107]](#cite_note-107)

In the city there is a Consulate of the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"),[[108]](#cite_note-108) a Consulate of the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives"),[[109]](#cite_note-109) and Honorary Consulates of [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany").[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)

### Utility services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Utility services")]

The [Kerala Water Authority](/wiki/Kerala_Water_Authority "Kerala Water Authority") supplies the city with water that is sourced from the [Karamana River](/wiki/Karamana_River "Karamana River");[[112]](#cite_note-water-112) most of it is drawn from the [Aruvikkara](/wiki/Aruvikkara "Aruvikkara") and [Peppara](/wiki/Peppara_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Peppara Wildlife Sanctuary") reservoirs, and it is treated and purified at the Aruvikkara pumping stations.[[113]](#cite_note-113) The Wellington Water Works, commissioned in 1933, is one of the oldest city water supply schemes in India.[[114]](#cite_note-114) The sewage water is treated at Muttathara sewage-treatment plant, which handles 32 million litres per day.[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116) The city area is divided into seven blocks for the execution of the sewage system.[[117]](#cite_note-117) Electricity is supplied by the [Kerala State Electricity Board](/wiki/Kerala_State_Electricity_Board "Kerala State Electricity Board").[[118]](#cite_note-118) Fire services are handled by the [Kerala Fire And Rescue Services](/wiki/Kerala_Fire_And_Rescue_Services "Kerala Fire And Rescue Services").[[119]](#cite_note-119)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Economy_of_Thiruvananthapuram "Economy of Thiruvananthapuram")

[![View of Technovalley, in the north western part of the city at night.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Trivandrum_City_North_Panorama.jpg/1280px-Trivandrum_City_North_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trivandrum_City_North_Panorama.jpg "View of Technovalley, in the northwestern part of the city at night.")

View of Technovalley, in the northwestern part of the city at night.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Thiruvananthapuram_Infosys_campus.JPG/250px-Thiruvananthapuram_Infosys_campus.JPG)](/wiki/File:Thiruvananthapuram_Infosys_campus.JPG)

A part of Infosys campus. Thiruvananthapuram is a major IT hub in India.

Thiruvananthapuram is strategically located just 10 nautical miles (19 km; 12 mi) away from the crucial Suez to Singapore Far East international shipping route, placing it in close proximity to key global maritime traffic. This advantageous location, combined with the availability of reliable electricity, fresh water, and a long coastline, has accelerated industrial growth in the city. Thiruvananthapuram's economy comprises [Information Technology](/wiki/Information_Technology "Information Technology"), education, plantations, aerospace, commerce and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism").[[120]](#cite_note-sceco-120) Thiruvananthapuram district contributes 10.31%, of the state's GDP.[[121]](#cite_note-ecrev-121) With an economic growth rate of 13.83%, Thiruvananthapuram is the fastest-growing district in Kerala.[[121]](#cite_note-ecrev-121) Thiruvananthapuram was listed as one of the top ten cities in India on Vibrancy and Consumption Index by a study conducted by global [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") firm [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley").[[122]](#cite_note-MorStan-122) State- and central-government employees make up a large percentage of the city's workforce.[[121]](#cite_note-ecrev-121)

[![A large container ship, seen through palm trees](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/MSC_IRINA_TRIVANDRUM_PORT_HD.jpg/250px-MSC_IRINA_TRIVANDRUM_PORT_HD.jpg)](/wiki/File:MSC_IRINA_TRIVANDRUM_PORT_HD.jpg)

Arrival of MSC *Irina*, the world's largest container ship, arriving at Trivandrum in June 2025, marking its first arrival at a South Asian port

Thiruvananthapuram is a major [aerospace](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace") research centre in India. The [Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre](/wiki/Vikram_Sarabhai_Space_Centre "Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre"), the most significant and leading centre of [ISRO](/wiki/Indian_Space_Research_Organisation "Indian Space Research Organisation"),[[123]](#cite_note-vssc-123) and several space-related, state-owned [ISRO centres](/wiki/Indian_Space_Research_Organisation#Organisation_structure "Indian Space Research Organisation") such as [Thumba Equatorial Rocket Launching Station](/wiki/Thumba_Equatorial_Rocket_Launching_Station "Thumba Equatorial Rocket Launching Station"), [Liquid Propulsion Systems Centre](/wiki/Liquid_Propulsion_Systems_Centre "Liquid Propulsion Systems Centre"), and [ISRO Inertial Systems Unit](/wiki/ISRO_Inertial_Systems_Unit "ISRO Inertial Systems Unit") are based in Thiruvananthapuram.[[124]](#cite_note-isrocen-124) The [BrahMos Aerospace Trivandrum Limited](/wiki/BrahMos_Aerospace "BrahMos Aerospace") is one of the leading missile integration and defence production units in India.[[125]](#cite_note-brahmos-125) Other enterprises include [Travancore Titanium Products](/wiki/Travancore_Titanium_Products "Travancore Titanium Products"), [Kerala Automobiles Limited](/wiki/Kerala_Automobiles_Limited "Kerala Automobiles Limited"), [MILMA](/wiki/Kerala_Co-operative_Milk_Marketing_Federation "Kerala Co-operative Milk Marketing Federation"), [English Indian Clays](/wiki/English_Indian_Clays "English Indian Clays"), [Keltron](/wiki/KELTRON "KELTRON"), [Trivandrum Rubber Works](/wiki/Trivandrum_Rubber_Works "Trivandrum Rubber Works") and [HLL Lifecare Limited](/wiki/HLL_Lifecare "HLL Lifecare").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Lulumalltrivandrum.jpg/250px-Lulumalltrivandrum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lulumalltrivandrum.jpg)

A highway at Thiruvananthapuram

Thiruvananthapuram is a major [IT](/wiki/Information_Technology "Information Technology") and [ITES](/wiki/Information_technology_enabled_services "Information technology enabled services") hub in India. The city contributes about 40-45% of Kerala's total software exports.[[121]](#cite_note-ecrev-121)[[126]](#cite_note-stone-126) Thiruvananthapuram houses major [multinational](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") [Technology companies](/wiki/Technology_company "Technology company") like [Oracle Corporation](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation"), [Nissan](/wiki/Nissan "Nissan"), [HCL Tech](/wiki/HCLTech "HCLTech"), [Accenture](/wiki/Accenture "Accenture"), [Allianz Technology](/wiki/Allianz "Allianz"), [Envestnet](/wiki/Envestnet "Envestnet"), [Tata Consultancy Services](/wiki/Tata_Consultancy_Services "Tata Consultancy Services"), [Infosys](/wiki/Infosys "Infosys"), [UST Global](/wiki/UST_Global "UST Global"), [Ernst & Young](/wiki/Ernst_%26_Young "Ernst & Young"), Flytxt, [Guidehouse](/wiki/Guidehouse "Guidehouse"), [Tata Elxsi](/wiki/Tata_Elxsi "Tata Elxsi"), [McKinsey & Company](/wiki/McKinsey_%26_Company "McKinsey & Company"), [RR Donnelley](/wiki/RR_Donnelley "RR Donnelley") and [Quest Global](https://www.questglobal.com/).[[127]](#cite_note-techcomp-127) [Technopark](/wiki/Technopark,_Trivandrum "Technopark, Trivandrum") is the largest information-technology park in India in terms of built-up area.[[128]](#cite_note-techlarge-128) It is the largest employment base campus in Kerala with 52,000 IT/ITES professionals and about 400 companies.[[121]](#cite_note-ecrev-121)[[127]](#cite_note-techcomp-127) Other IT, media and industrial campuses include [Kinfra Film and Video Park](/wiki/Kinfra_Film_and_Video_Park "Kinfra Film and Video Park"), Kinfra Apparel Park, B-HUB and [Chithranjali Film Complex](/wiki/Chithranjali_Film_Complex "Chithranjali Film Complex").[[129]](#cite_note-kinfra-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) Other major IT, [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") and industrial campuses include [Technocity](/wiki/Technocity,_Thiruvananthapuram "Technocity, Thiruvananthapuram"), [Bio 360 Life sciences park](/wiki/Bio_360_Life_Sciences_Park "Bio 360 Life Sciences Park") and [Digital Science Park](/wiki/Digital_Science_Park,_Thiruvananthapuram "Digital Science Park, Thiruvananthapuram").[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132)

Tourism is a significant economic sector.[[133]](#cite_note-Tourists_Statistics_2007-133)[[134]](#cite_note-tour-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) The presence of natural attractions like beaches, [backwaters](/wiki/Kerala_backwaters "Kerala backwaters"), hills, and plantations and attractions like heritage, history, [Ayurveda](/wiki/Ayurveda "Ayurveda"), medical tourism and knowledge centres attract many tourists.[[136]](#cite_note-trvtour-136) The city experienced a surge of investment in the real estate, infrastructure and retail sectors in 2016–17.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

Port-related activity is low mainly due to the underdevelopment of ports. [Vizhinjam International Seaport](/wiki/Vizhinjam_International_Seaport "Vizhinjam International Seaport") is India's first deep-water [transshipment](/wiki/Transshipment "Transshipment") Port.[[138]](#cite_note-138) Vizhinjam port's location is close to the international shipping routes and, it is just 10–12 nautical miles from the busy [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf")-[Malacca](/wiki/Strait_of_Malacca "Strait of Malacca") shipping lane.[[139]](#cite_note-139) The port also has a natural depth of 18 to 20 metres which can accommodate huge [container ships](/wiki/Container_ship "Container ship").[[140]](#cite_note-140) The berths at Vizhinjam port are designed to cater to vessels of up to 24,000 [TEU](/wiki/Twenty-foot_equivalent_unit "Twenty-foot equivalent unit").[[141]](#cite_note-141)

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Tourism_in_Thiruvananthapuram "Tourism in Thiruvananthapuram")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Harverting_lotus_leafs.jpg/250px-Harverting_lotus_leafs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harverting_lotus_leafs.jpg)

Harvesting lotus leaves from [Vellayani Lake](/wiki/Vellayani_Lake "Vellayani Lake").

Thiruvananthapuram is a major tourist hub in India.[[134]](#cite_note-tour-134) [Kovalam](/wiki/Kovalam "Kovalam") and [Varkala](/wiki/Varkala "Varkala") are popular beach towns near the city.[[142]](#cite_note-142) Other important beaches include [Poovar](/wiki/Poovar "Poovar"), [Shankumugham Beach](/wiki/Shankumugham_Beach "Shankumugham Beach"), Azhimala Beach, [Vizhinjam](/wiki/Vizhinjam "Vizhinjam") Beach and Veli Beach. Other places of interest include [Agasthyamala](/wiki/Agasthyamala "Agasthyamala") rain forests, [Neyyar Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Neyyar_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Neyyar Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Kallar](/wiki/Kallar,_Trivandrum "Kallar, Trivandrum"), Braemore, [Ponmudi](/wiki/Ponmudi "Ponmudi") hills, [Poovar](/wiki/Poovar "Poovar"), [Anchuthengu](/wiki/Anchuthengu "Anchuthengu") backwaters, Varkala Cliffs and [Kappil](/wiki/Kappil,_Thiruvananthapuram "Kappil, Thiruvananthapuram")-[Edava](/wiki/Edava "Edava") lakes.

The city is also known for its unique style of architecture involving [Kerala Architecture](/wiki/Architecture_of_Kerala "Architecture of Kerala") with British and Dravidian influences.[[143]](#cite_note-143) [Napier museum](/wiki/Napier_museum "Napier museum"), [Thiruvanathapuram Zoo](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Zoo "Thiruvananthapuram Zoo"), [Padmanabha Swamy temple](/wiki/Padmanabha_Swamy_temple "Padmanabha Swamy temple"), [Kuthira Malika palace](/wiki/Kuthira_Malika "Kuthira Malika"), Kilimanoor palace and The Thiruvananthapuram Golf Club heritage building are examples of this.

The main museums include [Kerala Science and Technology Museum](/wiki/Kerala_Science_and_Technology_Museum "Kerala Science and Technology Museum") (with its attached Priyadarsini [Planetarium](/wiki/Planetarium "Planetarium")), [Napier Museum](/wiki/Napier_Museum "Napier Museum"), [Kerala Soil Museum](/wiki/Kerala_Soil_Museum "Kerala Soil Museum") and [Koyikkal Palace Museum](/wiki/Koyikkal_Palace "Koyikkal Palace"). [Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Agasthyamala_Biosphere_Reserve "Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve") is listed in [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s [World Network of Biosphere Reserves](/wiki/World_Network_of_Biosphere_Reserves "World Network of Biosphere Reserves").[[144]](#cite_note-144)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Culture_of_Thiruvananthapuram "Culture of Thiruvananthapuram")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Napier_Museum_Thiruvananthapuran_DSW_New.jpg/250px-Napier_Museum_Thiruvananthapuran_DSW_New.jpg)](/wiki/File:Napier_Museum_Thiruvananthapuran_DSW_New.jpg)

Established in 1855, the [Napier Museum](/wiki/Napier_Museum "Napier Museum") contains a vast collection of Ancient paintings and archaeological artefacts

Thiruvananthapuram is known as the "Evergreen City of India" because of its green landscapes and the presence of many public parks.[[145]](#cite_note-145) Thiruvananthapuram has historically been a cultural hub in [Southern India](/wiki/South_India "South India") due to the development of arts, architecture and liberal customs by the rulers of erstwhile Thiruvananthapuram. As a testimony to this, renowned artists like Maharaja [Swathi Thirunal](/wiki/Swathi_Thirunal "Swathi Thirunal") and [Raja Ravi Varma](/wiki/Raja_Ravi_Varma "Raja Ravi Varma") hail from the city.[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147) Prominent social reformers such as [Sri Narayana Guru](/wiki/Narayana_Guru "Narayana Guru"), [Chattampi Swamikal](/wiki/Chattampi_Swamikal "Chattampi Swamikal"), [Ayyankali](/wiki/Ayyankali "Ayyankali"), [Vakkom Moulavi](/wiki/Vakkom_Moulavi "Vakkom Moulavi") and [C. V. Raman Pillai](/wiki/C._V._Raman_Pillai "C. V. Raman Pillai") also are from Thiruvananthapuram.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

Two of the three [Malayalam triumvirate poets](/wiki/Malayalam_triumvirate_poets "Malayalam triumvirate poets"), [Ulloor S. Parameswara Iyer](/wiki/Ulloor_S._Parameswara_Iyer "Ulloor S. Parameswara Iyer") and [Kumaran Asan](/wiki/Kumaran_Asan "Kumaran Asan") are from Thiruvananthapuram.[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150) Annual literature festivals like the Kovalam Literary Festival, are held in the city.[[151]](#cite_note-151) Literary development is further aided by state institutions such as the [State Central Library](/wiki/State_Central_Library,_Kerala "State Central Library, Kerala"), one of the oldest public libraries in India, which was established in 1829,[[152]](#cite_note-152) and other major libraries including the Thiruvananthapuram Corporation Central library, and the [Kerala University Library](/wiki/Kerala_University "Kerala University").[[153]](#cite_note-153) Thiruvananthapuram has been a hub of [classical music](/wiki/Indian_classical_music "Indian classical music") since the days of [Maharaja of Travancore](/wiki/List_of_maharajas_of_Travancore "List of maharajas of Travancore"), [Swathi Thirunal](/wiki/Swathi_Thirunal_Rama_Varma "Swathi Thirunal Rama Varma").[[154]](#cite_note-classicalmusictvm-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155) Thiruvananthapuram is known for many music festivals like the Navarathri Music Festival, one of the oldest festivals of its kind in South India,[[154]](#cite_note-classicalmusictvm-154) [Swathi Sangeethotsavam](/wiki/Swathi_Sangeethotsavam "Swathi Sangeethotsavam"), Soorya Music fest, Neelakanta Sivan Music Fest and many other music festivals are organised by various cultural groups.[[154]](#cite_note-classicalmusictvm-154) The 111-day-long [Soorya Festival](/wiki/Soorya_Festival "Soorya Festival") is the biggest art and cultural event in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala").[[156]](#cite_note-sooryafest-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157) The Soorya Festival features film festivals, theatre festivals, dance, music, painting and photography exhibitions.[[156]](#cite_note-sooryafest-156)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Iffk_2011_DSCN4617-crop.jpg/250px-Iffk_2011_DSCN4617-crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iffk_2011_DSCN4617-crop.jpg)

16th [IFFK](/wiki/IFFK "IFFK") 2011 banner at Kairali Theater Complex

The [Malayalam film Industry](/wiki/Malayalam_film_Industry "Malayalam film Industry") was started in Thiruvananthapuram. The first Malayalam feature film, [Vigathakumaran](/wiki/Vigathakumaran "Vigathakumaran") directed by [J. C. Daniel](/wiki/J._C._Daniel "J. C. Daniel") was released in Thiruvananthapuram.[[158]](#cite_note-158) J. C. Daniel is considered the father of Malayalam film industry. He also established the first film studio in Kerala, the Travancore National Pictures at Thiruvananthapuram in 1926.[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160) The [International Film Festival of Kerala](/wiki/International_Film_Festival_of_Kerala "International Film Festival of Kerala") (IFFK), which is held every year in December, is one of Asia's largest film festivals in terms of viewer participation.[[161]](#cite_note-161)[[162]](#cite_note-162) In addition to various film festivals, the presence of the [Central Board of Film Certification](/wiki/Central_Board_of_Film_Certification "Central Board of Film Certification")'s regional office, many movie studios and production facilities like the Uma Studio, [Chitranjali Studio](/wiki/Chitranjali_Studio "Chitranjali Studio"), [Merryland Studio](/wiki/Merryland_Studio "Merryland Studio"), [Kinfra Film and Video Park](/wiki/Kinfra_Film_and_Video_Park "Kinfra Film and Video Park") and Vismayas Max contribute to the growth of Thiruvananthapuram as a centre of cinema.[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-164)

Apart from the famous [Padmanabhaswamy Temple](/wiki/Padmanabhaswamy_Temple "Padmanabhaswamy Temple"), the city's architecture is championed by the [Napier Museum](/wiki/Napier_Museum "Napier Museum") and [Thiruvananthapuram Zoo](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Zoo "Thiruvananthapuram Zoo"), one of the oldest zoos in India.[[165]](#cite_note-165) Other architectural landmarks include [Kuthira Malika Palace](/wiki/Kuthira_Malika "Kuthira Malika"), [Kowdiar Palace](/wiki/Kowdiar_Palace "Kowdiar Palace"), [Attukal Bhagavathy Temple](/wiki/Attukal_Bhagavathy_Temple "Attukal Bhagavathy Temple"), [Beemapally Mosque](/wiki/Beemapally "Beemapally"), [Connemara Market](/wiki/Connemara_Market "Connemara Market"), and the [Mateer Memorial Church](/wiki/Mateer_Memorial_Church "Mateer Memorial Church"). Thiruvananthapuram was the main centre of [Laurie Baker](/wiki/Laurie_Baker "Laurie Baker")'s architecture.[[166]](#cite_note-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Attukal_pongala1.JPG/250px-Attukal_pongala1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Attukal_pongala1.JPG)

[Attukal Pongala](/wiki/Attukal_Pongala "Attukal Pongala") festival marks the world's largest gathering of women

Along with the major festivals of [Onam](/wiki/Onam "Onam"), [Vishu](/wiki/Vishu "Vishu"), [Deepavali](/wiki/Deepavali "Deepavali"), and [Navaratri](/wiki/Navaratri "Navaratri"), Christian and Islamic festivals like [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), [Eid ul-Fitr](/wiki/Eid_al-Fitr "Eid al-Fitr"), [Bakrid](/wiki/Bakrid "Bakrid") and [Milad-e-sheriff](/wiki/Mawlid "Mawlid"), the diverse ethnic populace of the city celebrates several local festivals like [Attukal Pongala](/wiki/Attukal_Pongala "Attukal Pongala"),[[167]](#cite_note-167) [Beemapally Uroos](/wiki/Beemapally "Beemapally"),[[168]](#cite_note-168) [Vettukaad Church Festival](/wiki/Mother_of_God_Church,_Vettukad "Mother of God Church, Vettukad"),[[169]](#cite_note-169) [Padmanabhaswamy Temple Aaraattu](/wiki/Padmanabhaswamy_Temple "Padmanabhaswamy Temple") and Lakshadeepam festival.[[170]](#cite_note-170) During the Onam festival, the state government conducts several cultural events for a week in the city.[[171]](#cite_note-171) The Attukal Pongala festival attracts millions of women devotees from across India and abroad. It is the largest gathering of women in the world.[[172]](#cite_note-atkp1-172)[[173]](#cite_note-atkp2-173) Germany's Goethe Zentrum, France's Alliance Française and Russia's Gorky Bhavan centres host a wide range of events and programmes throughout the year.[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175)[[176]](#cite_note-176)

### Fashion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Fashion")]

Thiruvananthapuram has a long-standing connection with textile traditions, especially through the [Balaramapuram Handlooms](/wiki/Balaramapuram_Handlooms "Balaramapuram Handlooms") industry. Known for its cotton fabrics with kasavu (gold zari borders), the region's weaving techniques have remained largely unchanged since the 18th century.[[177]](#cite_note-177) The handwoven fabrics continue to feature in both traditional attire and contemporary fashion collections, including international showcases.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

Modern fashion is also well-represented in the city, with shopping malls like Lulu Mall and Mall of Travancore hosting popular domestic and international clothing brands, reflecting current global trends in apparel and lifestyle.

## Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Trivandrum's cuisine reflects local food traditions shaped by its historical and cultural background, including influences from the former Travancore royal kitchens,[[179]](#cite_note-179) coastal trade, and religious diversity. The city's food culture includes a variety of vegetarian and non-vegetarian dishes, with rice, coconut, and spices forming the basis of most meals.

* **[Trivandrum Sadhya](/wiki/Trivandrum_Sadhya "Trivandrum Sadhya")**, a vegetarian meal served on a banana leaf, especially during festivals such as Onam and Vishu. It typically consists of multiple dishes including *sambar*, *kalan*, *avial*, *olan*, *pachadi*, varieties of *payasam* and Boli.[[180]](#cite_note-180)
* **[Trivandrum Boli](/wiki/Trivandrum_Boli "Trivandrum Boli")**, a traditional sweet dish made with lentils and flour, often served alongside desserts like *payasam*.
* **Kethel Chicken Fry**, from the year 1940, known for its preparation of deep-fried chicken served with small chapatis and onion salad.[[181]](#cite_note-181)
* **Vizhinjam Chicken Fry**, a dish from the Vizhinjam area of Trivandrum City, characterized by its use of local spices and cooking methods.[[182]](#cite_note-182)
* **[Travancore biriyani](/wiki/Travancore_biriyani "Travancore biriyani")**, is a rice-based dish from Trivandrum, rooted in the culinary traditions of the former Travancore. Distinct from other regional biryanis, it is spiced and emphasizes subtle flavors rather than heat. Prepared with long-grain rice, it incorporates local ingredients such as coconut, curry leaves, and regional spices. The dish reflects a blend of Trivandrum's indigenous cooking practices and Mughal culinary influences, resulting in a unique preparation that balances aromatic rice with delicately layered flavors.[[183]](#cite_note-183)
* **Travancore Prawns Curry** , is a dish with mild, balanced flavors from Trivandrum. It uses coconut milk, tamarind, and spices like black pepper and curry leaves, creating a less fiery taste compared to other regional seafood curries. The use of fresh, local ingredients gives it a distinct, subtle flavor profile[[184]](#cite_note-184)
* **Bakeries and Confectioneries:** Thiruvananthapuram has a long-standing tradition of bakeries and confectioneries, some of which have been operating for over a century. These establishments offer a variety of traditional and modern sweets, pastries, and cakes, reflecting the influence of European baking techniques adapted to local tastes. Some notable historical bakeries in the city include St. Michels, La Jawab, Melfe, and Salt n Pepper. These bakeries are known for their signature items like plum cakes, cream buns, and meat puffs, which have become an integral part of the city's culinary landscape.[[185]](#cite_note-185)

* **Trivandrum Tapioca Dishes**, Tapioca was introduced to India in Trivandrum, the capital of the former princely state of Travancore, during the reign of Maharaja Ayilyam Thirunal Rama Varma (1860–1880). It was introduced by him as part of efforts to address food shortages and improve agricultural resilience. Common tapioca dishes from the region include Kappa Puzhukku, Kappa Vevichathu, Kappa Meen Curry, Kappa Erachi (Kappa Biriyani), Kappa Ularthiyathu, Chenda Kappa, Kappa with Ulli Chammanthi, and Tapioca Chips [[186]](#cite_note-186)

Street food in Trivandrum includes items such as *kappa* (tapioca) with fish curry, *parippu vada*, *banana chips*, and *unniyappam*. Small eateries and roadside vendors are widespread throughout the city.The general cuisine of the people is [Keralite cuisine](/wiki/Kerala_cuisine "Kerala cuisine"), which is generally characterised by an abundance of [coconut](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut") and [spices](/wiki/Spice "Spice"). Other [South Indian](/wiki/South_Indian_culture#Cuisine "South Indian culture") cuisines, as well as [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine") and [North Indian](/wiki/Indian_cuisine "Indian cuisine") cuisines, are popular.[[187]](#cite_note-187) Thiruvananthapuram has many restaurants offering [Arabic](/wiki/Arab_cuisine "Arab cuisine"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine"), [Thai](/wiki/Thai_cuisine "Thai cuisine") and [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_cuisine "Mexican cuisine") cuisines.[[188]](#cite_note-188)[[189]](#cite_note-189)

## Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Health care")]

Thiruvananthapuram, the capital city of Kerala, has a robust healthcare system, with both government and private medical institutions offering comprehensive services. The Government Medical College, one of the oldest medical colleges in India, is a prominent center for medical education and healthcare. The [Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology](/wiki/Sree_Chitra_Tirunal_Institute_for_Medical_Sciences_and_Technology "Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology") (SCTIMST) is a well-known institution specializing in cardiology, neurology, and biomedical research. Other significant medical institutions include [Rajiv Gandhi Centre for Biotechnology](/wiki/Rajiv_Gandhi_Centre_for_Biotechnology "Rajiv Gandhi Centre for Biotechnology") (RGCB), which focuses on advanced biotechnological research, and [Kerala Institute of Medical Sciences](/wiki/Kerala_Institute_of_Medical_Sciences "Kerala Institute of Medical Sciences")[[190]](#cite_note-190) (KIMS), known for its multi-specialty services. Private hospitals like NIMS Hospital,[[191]](#cite_note-191) Aster capital, and Lord's Hospital[[192]](#cite_note-192) provide advanced care across various specialties, including cardiology, oncology, and orthopedics. Additionally, Ayurvedic treatment centers are prevalent in the city, offering traditional healing methods. The city also provides palliative care services, with initiatives like the Arike Home Daycare Program, which supports patients in need of end-of-life. care at home.

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Public transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Double_Decker_bus_in_Trivandrum.jpg/250px-Double_Decker_bus_in_Trivandrum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Double_Decker_bus_in_Trivandrum.jpg)

A [KSRTC](/wiki/Kerala_State_Road_Transport_Corporation "Kerala State Road Transport Corporation") Double-decker bus in the city

The majority of bus services are conducted by government operators. There are also private operators.[[206]](#cite_note-206) The city buses operated by [Kerala State Road Transport Corporation](/wiki/Kerala_State_Road_Transport_Corporation "Kerala State Road Transport Corporation") (KSRTC) are an important and reliable means of public transport available in the city. The main bus stations in the city are the [Central Bus Station](/wiki/Central_Bus_Station_Thiruvananthapuram "Central Bus Station Thiruvananthapuram") in [Thampanoor](/wiki/Thampanoor "Thampanoor"), where most of the long-distance buses ply from, and the city bus station in [East Fort](/wiki/East_Fort "East Fort"), where most city buses ply from.[[207]](#cite_note-207)[[208]](#cite_note-208) Three-wheeled, yellow and black [auto-rickshaws](/wiki/Auto-rickshaws "Auto-rickshaws") and taxis, are other popular forms of public transport.[[209]](#cite_note-209)[[210]](#cite_note-210) Thiruvananthapuram Metro is a fully elevated [metro rail](/wiki/Medium-capacity_rail_system "Medium-capacity rail system") – [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system planned to ease the congestion in the city.[[211]](#cite_note-tvmroad-211)[[212]](#cite_note-tvmmetro-212)

#### Metro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Metro")]

The [Thiruvananthapuram Metro](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Metro "Thiruvananthapuram Metro") is a proposed 42.1 km Conventional Metro rail system with 37 stations. With 2 primary lines connecting key hubs like Kazhakuttom and Karamana. The idea of the Thiruvananthapuram Metro was first proposed in the early 2000s to cater to the growing population. In 2025, a high-level committee led by the Chief Secretary was formed to examine and finalize the metro's alignment.[[213]](#cite_note-213) Three main routes under consideration are:

* Kazhakkoottam to Pappanamcode
* Kazhakkoottam to Killipalam
* Palayam to Civil Station

The alignment plan includes both underground and elevated sections.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Tvm_Metro.png/250px-Tvm_Metro.png)](/wiki/File:Tvm_Metro.png)

Proposed Trivandrum Metro route map

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Road")]

Thiruvananthapuram has a well-developed [road transport](/wiki/Road_transport "Road transport") infrastructure.[[211]](#cite_note-tvmroad-211) The roads in the city are maintained by the Thiruvananthapuram Roads Development Company Limited (TRDCL) and [Kerala PWD](/wiki/Kerala_Public_Works_Department "Kerala Public Works Department").[[214]](#cite_note-214) TRDCL manages the 42 km city roads which come under the Thiruvananthapuram City Roads Improvement Project (TRCIP), which is the first urban road project in India.[[215]](#cite_note-trcip-215)[[216]](#cite_note-216) TRCIP is a [public-private partnership](/wiki/Public%E2%80%93private_partnership "Public–private partnership") project to improve and maintain the existing road network in the city to cater to the needs of rapid urbanisation.[[215]](#cite_note-trcip-215) TRCIP has won the International Road Federation's Global Road Achievement Awards in 2015.[[217]](#cite_note-roadaward-217) TCRIP has also been selected by [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") as a replicable public-private partnership model.[[218]](#cite_note-unroad-218) It was one of the 12 [public-private partnership](/wiki/Public%E2%80%93private_partnership "Public–private partnership") project case studies from across the world which fulfil the [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals") of the UN Agenda 2030.[[219]](#cite_note-unroad1-219)[[220]](#cite_note-unroad2-220) In 2024, Thiruvananthapuram became the first Indian city to win the UN global sustainability award.[[221]](#cite_note-221)

Thiruvananthapuram is served by [National Highway 66](/wiki/National_Highway_66_(India) "National Highway 66 (India)") of India's [National Highways system](/wiki/National_Highway_(India) "National Highway (India)").[[222]](#cite_note-222) The city is connected to the [North-South Corridor](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_and_East%E2%80%93West_Corridor "North–South and East–West Corridor") of the National Highway system at [Aralvaimozhi](/wiki/Aralvaimozhi "Aralvaimozhi"), which is 80 km south of the city.[[223]](#cite_note-223) The [State Highway 1](/wiki/State_Highway_1_(Kerala) "State Highway 1 (Kerala)"), which commonly known as the [Main Central Road](/wiki/Main_Central_Road "Main Central Road") is an arterial highway in the city. Other major highways in the city are [State Highway 2](/wiki/State_Highway_2_(Kerala) "State Highway 2 (Kerala)") and [State Highway 45](/wiki/State_Highway_45_(Kerala) "State Highway 45 (Kerala)").[[224]](#cite_note-stateroads-224) The [Mahatma Gandhi Road](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi_Road_(Thiruvananthapuram) "Mahatma Gandhi Road (Thiruvananthapuram)") is the main arterial road in the city.[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-226) Another important road is the Kowdiar Road, which is also known as the Royal Road, as it leads to the [Kowdiar Palace](/wiki/Kowdiar_Palace "Kowdiar Palace").[[227]](#cite_note-227)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Rail")]

Thiruvananthapuram is a [divisional headquarters](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_railway_division "Thiruvananthapuram railway division") in the [Southern Railway zone](/wiki/Southern_Railway_zone "Southern Railway zone") of the [Indian Railways](/wiki/Indian_Railways "Indian Railways").[[228]](#cite_note-228) Long-distance trains originate from [Thiruvananthapuram Central](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Central_railway_station "Thiruvananthapuram Central railway station") and [Thiruvananthapuram North](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_North_railway_station "Thiruvananthapuram North railway station") railway terminals. Kochuveli railway terminal is developed to ease congestion on the central station and it acts as a satellite station to Thiruvananthapuram Central.[[229]](#cite_note-229) [Thiruvananthapuram Central](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Central_railway_station "Thiruvananthapuram Central railway station") is the busiest railway station in Kerala.[[230]](#cite_note-230) Other railway stations in the city are [Thiruvananthapuram North](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_North_railway_station "Thiruvananthapuram North railway station"), [Thiruvananthapuram Pettah](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Pettah "Thiruvananthapuram Pettah"), [Thiruvananthapuram South railway station](/wiki/Nemom_railway_station "Nemom railway station"), [Veli railway station](/wiki/Veli_Railway_Station "Veli Railway Station") and [Kazhakoottam railway station](/wiki/Kazhakoottam_railway_station "Kazhakoottam railway station"). Being the southernmost [municipal corporation](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_in_India "Municipal Corporations in India") in India, many long train services of Indian Railways originate from Thiruvananthapuram like Thiruvananthapuram Rajdhani Express, [Thiruvananthapuram - Silchar Superfast Express](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_-_Silchar_Superfast_Express "Thiruvananthapuram - Silchar Superfast Express") and [Thiruvananthapuram North - Amritsar Weekly Express](/wiki/Kochuveli_-_Amritsar_Weekly_Express "Kochuveli - Amritsar Weekly Express"). There are plans to develop a railway terminal at [Thiruvananthapuram South railway station](/wiki/Nemom_railway_station "Nemom railway station") to reduce congestion at [Thiruvananthapuram Central](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Central_railway_station "Thiruvananthapuram Central railway station").[[231]](#cite_note-231)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Air")]

Thiruvananthapuram is served by the [Thiruvananthapuram International Airport](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_International_Airport "Thiruvananthapuram International Airport"), located at Chakka, only 6.7 km (4.2 mi) from the city centre. The airport started operations in 1935 and is the first airport in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala").[[232]](#cite_note-232) Being one of the gateways to the state, it has direct connectivity to all the major cities in India as well as the Middle East, Malaysia, Singapore, the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"). As the city is headquarters of the [Southern Air Command](/wiki/Southern_Air_Command_(India) "Southern Air Command (India)") (SAC) of the [Indian Air Force](/wiki/Indian_Air_Force "Indian Air Force"), Thiruvananthapuram International Airport caters to the Indian Air Force (IAF) and the Coast Guard for their strategic operations.[[233]](#cite_note-Southern_Air_Command,_Thiruvananthapuram-233) IAF has an exclusive apron to handle all their operations. The airport also caters to the [Rajiv Gandhi Academy for Aviation Technology](/wiki/Rajiv_Gandhi_Academy_for_Aviation_Technology "Rajiv Gandhi Academy for Aviation Technology") which carries out pilot-training activities.[[234]](#cite_note-234)

### Sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thiruvananthapuram&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Sea")]

Small cruise ships often dock at Vizhinjam Harbour.[[235]](#cite_note-235) A [cruise terminal](/wiki/Ferry_terminal "Ferry terminal") is under trial run at [Vizhinjam Transshipment Terminal](/wiki/Vizhinjam_International_Seaport "Vizhinjam International Seaport") and some of the world's largest container vessels had already docked here.[[236]](#cite_note-236)[[237]](#cite_note-237) Vizhinjam seaport has been designated by the government as an authorised immigration check-post for entry and exit from India for international ships and cruises.[[238]](#cite_note-238)
